briefing.opening: Hello everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 29, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Record Heatwaves and the Realization of the Climate Crisis
sections.content: Europe is facing unprecedented record heatwaves exceeding 40 degrees Celsius for the first time, resulting in severe human casualties, including over 1,300 deaths [11, 70, 71, 81]. The direct impacts of climate change are accelerating, with Swiss glaciers losing all their winter snow by late June [15], and one-fifth of the Pine Island Glacier's retreat attributed to climate change [53]. Experts warn that such extreme heatwaves would have been impossible without climate change [70, 77, 80], pointing out that climate change is reshaping overall social structures and environments, including childhood, fisheries, and the tourism industry [52, 61, 75]. The urgency of climate crisis adaptation is further highlighted as some European regions, including Ukraine, have announced temporary usage restrictions in preparation for the upcoming heatwaves' impact on energy networks [82].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Climate Change Response Efforts and Innovations
sections.content: Domestically, the 'Climate and Climate Change Monitoring and Prediction Act,' aimed at strengthening the scientific basis for regional climate crisis response, has been proposed [4], and the Ministry of Climate continues its policy efforts, including supporting the expansion of the greenhouse gas reduction awareness budget system [10]. Educational programs for future generations, such as Muan Office of Education's 'AI Climate Change Project' [6] and Yongin Special City's 'Climate Crisis Adaptation Special Program' [8], are being implemented. Efforts to digitally prevent complex disasters like floods and heatwaves [9] and to seek solutions through low-carbon diets [13] are also active. Amidst continued corporate participation, including KAI's declaration of advanced ESG management [5] and Lotte Chilsung Beverage's acceleration of plastic reduction and carbon neutrality [21], the appointment of Professor Bae Choong-sik, a carbon neutrality and future energy researcher, as the new president of KAIST [19, 20, 24] is expected to strengthen expertise in related fields. Internationally, positive grassroots changes have also been reported, with Scottish volunteers collecting over 11 million seeds to restore rainforests [83].

sections.title: ⚠️ Opportunities and Challenges in Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The government is planning investments totaling 2,000 trillion Korean Won in 'three mega-projects,' including AI data centers and semiconductors, and is pursuing large-scale renewable energy adoption, aiming to secure 100 GW of renewable energy to support these initiatives [36, 37]. The Korea Energy Agency is recruiting participants for a pilot project of an intermediary platform to activate direct renewable energy PPAs (Power Purchase Agreements) [33, 35, 43], working to resolve information asymmetry in the PPA contract market [31]. However, in the United States, investments worth $121 billion are at risk due to delays in renewable energy permitting [30], and domestically, concerns have been raised that the southwestern semiconductor project faces limitations in providing stable 24-hour power supply solely through renewable energy [32, 38, 45, 48]. The establishment of a legal framework for replacing and improving the performance of aging solar and wind power facilities is being pursued [40], and technological advancements, such as the trend of battery-coupled energy contracts replacing standalone solar and wind power generation [39], demonstrate the potential to address these challenges.

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Policy Dilemmas and Perception Gaps
sections.content: The international community's efforts to address climate change are facing dilemmas. The World Bank is set to abandon its goal of allocating 45% of its lending resources to climate change projects [51, 64]. In the United States, concerns about policy setbacks are rising, with New York's climate change education bill failing [54] and the Trump administration showing signs of shortening public comment periods for fossil fuel drilling on federal lands and allowing more warming emissions [85]. Domestically, the 'third warming gas,' not included in official statistics, has been identified as a hidden culprit of climate change [1], and criticism has been raised regarding the omission of climate change-related content from the revised Stewardship Code [2], indicating many areas still requiring improvement. Amidst these circumstances, the German President emphasized that international norms are crucial for climate change response [59], and the FIFA World Cup was also credited with elevating climate change onto the global agenda [66].

briefing.closing: Today's climate briefing has once again highlighted the reality of climate change, underscored by the record-breaking heatwaves sweeping across the globe. It also shed light on the diverse response efforts underway both domestically and internationally, as well as the complex challenges of transitioning to renewable energy. Securing international solidarity and policy consistency to address the climate crisis is an urgent priority. This concludes the climate briefing for June 29, 2026.

articles.description: Red warnings issued in Hungary, Poland, Romania and the Balkans, with authorities urging people to stay indoorsParts of central, eastern and southern Europe sweltered on Monday as the “heat dome” behind last week’s record-breaking temperatures shifted east, bringing dangerous conditions to a new swathe of the continent.Budapest is forecast to exceed 40C on Tuesday, according to models from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts. Continue reading...

summary.phenomenon: On Monday, dangerous heatwaves occurred in parts of Central, Eastern, and Southern Europe, including Hungary, Poland, Romania, and the Balkans. Budapest is forecast to exceed 40C on Tuesday. Red warnings have been issued for these regions.
summary.cause: This is due to the 'heat dome' that caused last week's record-breaking temperatures shifting eastward.
summary.outlook: Authorities have urged residents to stay indoors due to the dangerous temperatures.

summary.phenomenon: Ukraine is bracing for the impact of the upcoming heatwave on its energy network. Grid operators in at least five regions, from Ivano-Frankivsk in the west to Zaporizhzhia on the southern frontline, have announced temporary restrictions on energy usage for parts of Tuesday.
summary.cause: Ukraine's energy network has already been severely damaged by over four years of Russian attacks, with the heatwave posing an additional burden.
summary.outlook: Temporary restrictions on energy usage are expected to be implemented for parts of Tuesday in the affected regions.

articles.original_title: ‘Literally growing the future’: volunteers help save Scottish rainforest by collecting 11m seeds
articles.description: Teams painstakingly combed endangered Atlantic habitat over several years, helping to grow 8m native treesA small band of volunteers has helped to grow nearly 8m native trees in Scotland, crucial to efforts to restore lost parts of the Atlantic rainforest, after collecting 11m seeds by hand.About 100 volunteers, including retired teachers and doctors, office workers and young families, have spent tens of thousands of hours venturing into often remote woods in the western Highlands and islands to

summary.phenomenon: In Scotland, volunteers have contributed to growing nearly 8 million native trees by hand-collecting 11 million seeds over several years. This activity took place in endangered Atlantic habitats, such as remote woods in the Western Highlands and islands.
summary.cause: These efforts are part of a crucial initiative to restore lost parts of the Atlantic rainforest.
summary.outlook: The volunteers' activities are contributing to the restoration of Scotland's lost Atlantic rainforest.

articles.title: Trump officials to slash public input on fossil fuel drilling on federal lands
articles.description: Plan to limit scrutiny of polluters and shift financial risks to taxpayers is attack on democracy, advocates sayThe Trump administration is attempting to shrink public comment periods for fossil fuel leasing on federal land while shifting the financial risks of cleanup to taxpayers and allowing for more planet-warming emissions. summary.outlook: Advocates argue that this plan is an attack on democracy, limiting scrutiny of polluters, shifting financial risks to taxpayers, and allowing for more planet-warming emissions.
